title: Beyond Single-Image Augmentation
concept: Traditional augmentations like flips, crops, and color shifts only modify one image at a time. Multi-image augmentation techniques blend or combine multiple images together, forcing the model to learn more robust decision boundaries.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary difference between geometric and photometric augmentation?

options.text: Geometric moves pixel positions (rotation, crop); photometric changes pixel values (brightness, color)
Geometric is only for validation data; photometric is for training data
Geometric changes the label; photometric does not

exercises.explain: Yes! Geometric edits where pixels sit; photometric edits color and brightness values.

cards.title: The One-at-a-Time Limit
cards.body: Traditional data augmentation (flips, crops, color jitter) is applied to individual images in isolation. A cat photo stays a cat photo. But what if we could combine features of two completely different images? This might sound strange, but it teaches the network to generalize in ways single-image tricks never can.

cards.title: Multi-Image Augmentation
cards.body: Multi-image augmentation combines two or more different training images (like a cat and a dog) to create a synthetic training sample. Critically, we also blend their labels proportionally, teaching the model to output a partial confidence instead of forcing a 100% decision.

cards.title: Mixing a fruit smoothie
cards.body: Single-image augmentation is like slicing an apple or peeling an orange — you change its shape, but it's still just an apple or an orange. Multi-image augmentation is like blending them into a smoothie. It's now 70% apple and 30% orange, forcing you to recognize both flavors in the same cup.

cards.title: Synthetic training pairs
cards.body: By blending a cat and a dog image, we create a composite image. We also blend their target labels: the model is taught that this new blended image is 0.6 Cat and 0.4 Dog. This prevents the model from becoming overconfident.

cards.title: It's not just a visual trick
cards.body: It's easy to assume this is just about making weird images. The real magic is in the labels. By forcing the model to predict soft target probabilities (like 60% cat, 40% dog) rather than absolute 1s and 0s, we prevent it from overfitting and make it much more robust to real-world uncertainty.

cards.title: Single-Image vs. Multi-Image
cards.body: How the two augmentation philosophies differ:

left.text:
Modifies 1 image in isolation
Label remains 100% Cat
Teaches spatial/light invariance

right.text:
Combines Cat + Dog images
Label becomes 60% Cat / 40% Dog
Smooths decision boundaries

exercises.prompt: What is the key difference between single-image augmentation and multi-image augmentation?

options.text: Multi-image blends multiple training samples and their labels proportionally
Multi-image only works on grayscale images
Multi-image is applied only during testing and evaluation
Single-image augmentation completely replaces the network's layers

exercises.explain: Correct! Multi-image techniques combine multiple inputs and blend their corresponding labels.

exercises.prompt: True or false: When combining a cat and a dog image, the final target label remains 100% cat.
exercises.explain: False. The label must be blended proportionally to match the ratio of the combined images.

exercises.sentence: Multi-image augmentation teaches the model to predict soft, ___ labels instead of absolute 100% confidences.
exercises.explain: Proportional labels match the input blend ratio, preventing overconfident predictions.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a benefit of forcing a CNN to learn from blended images with soft labels?

options.text: It helps the model generalize better by smoothing out its decision boundaries
It decreases the number of parameters in the convolutional layers
It guarantees that the model will train in under one epoch

exercises.explain: Correct! Soft labels smooth out the model's decision boundaries, preventing overfitting and boosting generalization.

exercises.prompt: Why does training a CNN on absolute labels (always 1.0 or 0.0) sometimes lead to poor real-world generalization?

options.text: It encourages the model to become highly overconfident, making extreme mistakes on ambiguous or noisy real-world images
It makes the gradients vanish entirely during the first epoch
It forces the model to ignore all convolutional filter weights
There is no difference; models generalize identically with absolute or soft labels

exercises.explain: Exactly! Absolute labels encourage overconfidence. Next, let's look at the first famous multi-image method: Mixup!
